disease.name: Hepatitis C
disease.note: Flavivirus (HCV) predominantly causing chronic hepatitis. Six major genotypes (1-6) with genotype 1 most common globally. DAA therapy now achieves >95% SVR. Leading indication for liver transplant before DAAs.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute liver failure/decompensation -> EMERGENCY; Compensated chronic HCV -> URGENT DAA treatment; SVR after therapy -> SURVEILLANCE if cirrhotic

historyFeatures.note: Only 15% of acute cases icteric; most acute infections subclinical

subtypes.name: Acute Hepatitis C
subtypes.note: Incubation 14-180 days. 70-80% asymptomatic. Only 15-30% develop jaundice. 15-45% spontaneously clear (typically within 12 weeks). Spontaneous clearance is rare after 6 months.

subtypes.name: Chronic Hepatitis C
subtypes.note: HCV RNA + >6 months. 55-85% of infections become chronic. Silent progression over 20-30 years. 15-30% develop cirrhosis within 20 years (higher with alcohol, male, HIV, steatosis).

subtypes.name: HCV Genotype 1 (1a, 1b)
subtypes.note: Most common in North America, Europe, Japan (60-70%). Historically hardest to treat (IFN-based). Modern pangenotypic DAAs equally effective.

subtypes.name: HCV Genotype 2
subtypes.note: More common in West Africa, Italy. Good response to therapy. Pangenotypic regimens effective. 8 weeks often sufficient for non-cirrhotic.

subtypes.name: HCV Genotype 3
subtypes.note: Common in South Asia (India, Pakistan). More steatogenic, faster fibrosis progression. Some DAAs less effective (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir still highly effective).

subtypes.name: HCV Genotype 4
subtypes.note: Most common in Middle East and Africa (especially Egypt). Pangenotypic DAAs effective.

subtypes.name: HCV Genotypes 5 and 6
subtypes.note: Rarer. Genotype 5 in South Africa, genotype 6 in Southeast Asia. Pangenotypic regimens cover all genotypes.

laboratory.name: Anti-HCV antibody (EIA/CIA)
laboratory.indication: screening test; may be negative for first 4-10 weeks (window period) and in immunocompromised

laboratory.name: HCV RNA quantitative (PCR)
laboratory.indication: confirms active infection; detectable as early as 1-2 weeks post-exposure; used for treatment monitoring

laboratory.name: HCV genotype
laboratory.indication: guide DAA regimen selection (although pangenotypic agents have reduced need)

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: ALT often waxes/wanes; normal ALT does NOT exclude significant fibrosis

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; elevated in decompensated cirrhosis

laboratory.name: Serum albumin
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; low albumin suggests advanced disease

laboratory.name: AFP
laboratory.indication: HCC surveillance if cirrhosis present; baseline before DAA therapy

laboratory.name: HBsAg, Anti-HBs, Anti-HBc
laboratory.indication: screen for HBV coinfection before DAA therapy (risk of HBV reactivation)

laboratory.name: HIV serology
laboratory.indication: check for coinfection given shared risk factors

laboratory.name: Cryoglobulins, rheumatoid factor, complement C3/C4
laboratory.indication: evaluate for mixed cryoglobulinemia if extrahepatic symptoms

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: liver morphology, steatosis, cirrhosis, HCC screening

imaging.name: Transient elastography (FibroScan)
imaging.indication: fibrosis staging essential before DAA therapy; rule out advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis

imaging.name: CT abdomen triphasic or MRI
imaging.indication: HCC characterization if mass found; pre-transplant assessment if decompensated

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: rarely needed in DAA era; reserved for discordant non-invasive tests or suspected co-pathology

management.protocolReference: AASLD/IDSA HCV Guidance; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Hepatitis C

pathways.criteria: Recent HCV exposure (weeks-to-3 months), detectable HCV RNA, ALT may be elevated
pathways.treatment: Option 1: Observe for spontaneous clearance for 12-16 weeks (40% will clear). Option 2: Early DAA therapy (preferred in genotype 3, symptomatic, or high-risk populations). Pangenotypic regimen: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 400/100mg PO daily x 8 weeks (non-cirrhotic). If genotype 1b, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 300/120mg PO daily x 8 weeks.

pathways.criteria: HCV RNA+ >6 months, no cirrhosis on FibroScan (F0-F2, liver stiffness <12.5 kPa)
pathways.treatment: Pangenotypic DAA regimen: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 400/100mg PO daily x 12 weeks (or 8 weeks if genome 1/2 with low viral load). Alternative: glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 300/120mg PO daily x 8 weeks (all genotypes). Obtain SVR12 (HCV RNA 12 weeks post-treatment). HCV cure defined as SVR12.

pathways.criteria: HCV RNA+, FibroScan liver stiffness ≥12.5 kPa (F4), Child-Pugh A, no decompensation
pathways.treatment: Pangenotypic DAA: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 400/100mg PO daily x 12 weeks (no ribavirin); OR glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 300/120mg PO daily x 12 weeks. For genotype 3 with cirrhosis, consider adding weight-based ribavirin 1000-1200mg/day PO divided BID. HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m indefinitely (HCC risk persists even after SVR). Variceal screening EGD.

pathways.criteria: Child-Pugh B or C, ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy, variceal bleeding
pathways.treatment: Refer to transplant center. DAA therapy in decompensated cirrhosis: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 400/100mg PO daily + ribavirin (weight-based: 600mg if <75kg, 1000-1200mg if >75kg, start low and titrate) x 12 weeks. Alternatively: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir/voxilaprevir not studied in decompensated. Avoid protease inhibitors in Child-Pugh B/C. Monitor for MELD improvement; some may improve enough to delist.

pathways.criteria: HCV RNA+, HIV+, on or off ART
pathways.treatment: Same DAA regimens as HCV monoinfection. Check for drug-drug interactions with ART. Generally safe with tenofovir alafenamide, integrase inhibitors, and most NNRTIs. Avoid efavirenz with some DAAs (reduces DAA levels). Monitor renal function if on TDF.

disease.emergencyPriority: low for chronic HCV; medium for acute liver failure; high for decompensated cirrhosis
